title: Uganda Information Security (DEMO - Historical)
description: Operational security during authoritarian surveillance. State Research Bureau infiltrated communities. Informants were everywhere.
items: Assume all phone calls are monitored - State Research Bureau had extensive wiretap capabilities
Do not discuss plans in public spaces - restaurants, shops, buses all had informants
Avoid political discussions entirely - criticism of Amin or military could result in arrest, torture, or death
Letters and mail were opened - do not write anything sensitive. Use coded language if necessary.
Trust only immediate family - and even then, compartmentalize information. What they don't know can't be extracted.
Burn or destroy sensitive documents before leaving - anything left behind could be used against remaining community members
Do not announce departure date publicly - made you vulnerable to robbery, extortion, or detention
Servants and employees could be informants - even long-trusted staff faced pressure to report on employers
Radio Uganda was propaganda - only official source of news and completely unreliable
BBC World Service - listened to secretly after dark. Provided real information about situation in Uganda.
Community gatherings monitored - temples, mosques, and religious events were infiltrated by security forces
Photography was dangerous - cameras were confiscated, film destroyed. Being photographed could mark you as target.
Foreign journalists were expelled or arrested - independent reporting was eliminated by 1973
